content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: A number of verbs require a preposition before the -ing form.
examples: Lucia insisted **on arriving** early to the meeting.
We're looking forward **to visiting** the new exhibition.

content.subtitle: Verb + object + -ing form
examples: She caught the child drawing on the wall.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: The verbs feel, hear, see, notice, overhear and watch may also take an object followed by the bare infinitive (without to).
examples: I saw Victor **cross** the street. (= the whole action)
I saw Victor **crossing** the street. (= part of the action)

content.subtitle: Different subjects
content.description: If the verb and the gerund have different subjects, either an object pronoun or a possessive pronoun can be used for clarity.
examples: Would you mind **me/my** borrowing your notes for the exam?

content.subtitle: Verbs followed by full infinitive
examples: Can she afford to rent a flat in that area?

content.subtitle: Verbs followed by object + full infinitive
examples: My colleague advised me to speak to the manager directly.

content.subtitle: Verbs followed by object + bare infinitive
examples: The coach let the players finish training early.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: In passive forms, make is followed by the full infinitive.
examples: Dad **made me apologise** to my brother.
I was **made to apologise** to my brother.

content.subtitle: Modal uses
content.description: The verbs dare and need can be used as modals, in which case they are followed by the bare infinitive.
examples: I don't **dare tell** Lucas what happened.
You **needn't inform** Clara if you'd rather not.

content.subtitle: Little or no change in meaning
Change in meaning
items: I've always considered her to be a mentor.
We're considering moving to a different city.
**forget** + to-infinitive (not do something you were planning to do)
I forgot to ask Nadia about the appointment.
**forget** + -ing (not be able to remember a past event)
I'll never forget asking the professor that question.
**go on** + to-infinitive (stop one action and start another)
We discussed the project and then she went on to outline her long-term vision.
How can you go on working in that environment?
**like** + to-infinitive (be in the habit of doing; think it right to do)
I like to review my schedule at the start of each week.
I don't like being dismissed in such an abrupt way.
I'm sure Tomás didn't mean to offend anyone.
Being a team leader means always being accountable for your decisions.
**regret** + to-infinitive (be sorry about giving someone bad news)
We regret to inform you that the vacancy has been filled.
Do you regret leaving the company so early?
**remember** + to-infinitive (do something you are/were planning to do)
Did you remember to book the meeting room?
I don't remember requesting any additional feedback.
**stop** + to-infinitive (interrupt an action to do something else)
Why didn't you stop to consider the consequences first?
Would you please just stop interrupting me during presentations?
**try** + to-infinitive (make an effort to achieve something)
Try not to miss the submission deadline.
**try** + -ing (do something as an experiment to solve a problem)
You could try sending a written apology instead.

content.subtitle: Preparatory it
content.description: With some verbs, such as find, think or consider, it is often possible to use it as a preparatory object.
examples: I consider it remarkable that the renovation was completed ahead of schedule.

content.subtitle: Form and use
content.description: The subjunctive is a verb form that remains unchanged in the third person singular (no -s). It is used in that-clauses following expressions indicating necessity or preference.
examples: The therapist suggested that Rosa **take** a short break from her studies.
It is very important that Carlos **not hear** about this plan.
It's absolutely essential that **I be** contacted as soon as the delegation arrives.

content.subtitle: Alternative
content.description: An alternative is to use should in place of the subjunctive.
examples: The therapist suggested that Rosa (should) take a short break from her studies.

sections.title: A: Write the correct form of the verb
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: 1 The sports club's committee is considering ...... (allow) non-members to attend the monthly training sessions.
2 They simply can't afford ...... (get) new laboratory equipment this financial year.
3 I don't recall ...... (have) any problems with this system before today.
4 It's no use ...... (sleep) on a mattress that old – your back will suffer.
5 After all her hard work, she deserves ...... (know) the outcome of the investigation.
6 He can't help ...... (feel) nervous whenever he has to present to a large audience.
7 She didn't hesitate ...... (apply) for the overseas fellowship despite the tough competition.
8 He has arranged ...... (join) the advanced pottery class beginning next Tuesday.
9 The back garden clearly needs ...... (tidy) before the estate agent comes to value the property.
10 I was about to leave the gallery when I happened ...... (bump) into an old college friend.
11 Our coach never allowed us ...... (help) with setting up the equipment before competitions.
12 I strongly urge you ...... (contact) your insurance company before taking any further steps.

sections.title: B: Circle the correct verb form
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Mia: Oh, hello! You must be Felix. Felix: That's right! Mia, is it? I have to admit (1) **to be / to being** a little intimidated. I've never been to a book club before. Mia: Neither have I! I almost didn't come (2) **to see / seeing** what it was like. But you just have to risk (3) **to meet / meeting** people you might not connect with, don't you? Felix: I suppose so. Do you mind (4) **to sit / sitting** near the back? I feel more comfortable there. Mia: That's fine with me. It was my flatmate, Rosa, who suggested (5) **to meet / meeting** you. Felix: Really? Did she happen (6) **to tell / telling** you which books I'd recommended? Mia: She mentioned you enjoy literary fiction, but she refused (7) **to say / saying** anything more. Felix: Fair enough. Well, I've been looking forward (8) **to meet / to meeting** you. Do you feel like (9) **to order / ordering** a coffee before we start? Mia: Yes, please! I think I fancy (10) **to have / having** a cappuccino. What about you? Felix: Great idea. Let me find the waiter.
items.answer: 1 to being, 2 to see, 3 meeting, 4 sitting, 5 meeting, 6 to tell, 7 to say, 8 to meeting, 9 ordering, 10 having

sections.title: C: Complete using the verbs in the box in the correct form
sections.instructions: Complete using the verbs in the box in the correct form. You may need to add a preposition. (accept, apply, ask, commit, delay, fine, promote, replace, submit, wait)

items.sentence: 1 How can you justify ...... the announcement for so long?
2 All board members voted ...... the revised budget without further discussion.
3 The contractors have undertaken ...... all defective parts by the end of the month.
4 The executive was accused ...... financial irregularities during the audit.
5 The driver claimed ...... unfairly by the traffic warden, but the recording showed otherwise.
6 We simply cannot afford ...... any longer – the funding window closes tomorrow.
7 The initiative aims to encourage local residents ...... planning objections through the official portal.
8 Why do you keep ...... me about what the finance director decided?
9 I challenge any of you ...... for this grant and not find it worthwhile.
10 The deputy manager was quietly hoping ...... before the annual review.

sections.title: D: Correct the incorrect form
sections.instructions: One verb in each sentence is in the wrong form. Underline the incorrect form and rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: 1 She was grateful for all the support she received when she volunteered to help Lena finish **to organise** the annual fundraiser.
2 The firm has committed to boosting sales this quarter and intends **avoiding** any unnecessary expenditure in the months ahead.
3 Working abroad involves **to adapt** to new cultural norms and being open to learning different ways of doing things.
4 Marco always appears to want to participate in workshops but he needs to avoid **to be** so hesitant or he will never manage to get his ideas across.
5 Nutritionists suggest reducing sugar intake, so consider switching to healthier alternatives or, if you can't face **to give up** your favourite treats entirely, try cutting back gradually.
6 I hate being kept waiting at appointments, and it tends **happening** at this clinic more than anywhere else, so please don't give me any more excuses.
7 What prompted Elena **applying** for the research programme abroad was that her mentor expected her to find an opportunity that promised to enhance her academic profile.
8 Many aspiring writers dream of publishing novels, but few are prepared to endure **to spend** years developing their craft and honing their style.
9 I dislike having to pretend **getting** along with colleagues when they don't bother to make any effort to build a working relationship.
10 If you are thinking of enrolling on the journalism course at Westfield College, we suggest you **to speak** to the admissions tutor, who needs **knowing** which pathway you intend to follow.

sections.title: E: Complete with a pair of verbs
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence with a pair of verbs from the box in the correct form. (catch / wish, feel / follow, find / hide, glimpse / walk, hear / say, notice / try, observe / interact, see / come, smell / burn, watch / climb)

items.sentence: 1 I couldn't be certain, but I thought I ...... Clara ...... past the library window.
2 Did you ...... the director ...... that he was thinking of relocating the head office?
3 During the research session, the scientists ...... the dolphins ...... playfully with each other.
4 Did you leave the iron on? I can ...... something ...... upstairs.
5 I searched the whole flat and eventually ...... the kitten ...... behind the wardrobe.
6 We ...... the rescue team ...... up the collapsed building to reach the trapped survivors.
7 I couldn't help ...... the technician ...... to connect the projector before the presentation began.
8 She ...... someone ...... her along the corridor, but when she turned around there was nobody there.
9 He ...... himself ...... he had never accepted the invitation to join that project.
10 I ...... Dr Reyes ...... towards me across the car park, so I quickly slipped into the nearest café.

sections.title: F: Underline and correct the wrong verbs
sections.instructions: Underline twelve verbs in the wrong form and rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: The rise of remote working over the past decade has led to many more employees deciding **getting** their tasks done from home. New communication technologies have let companies **to allow** flexible arrangements to flourish and, despite having promised **staying** physically present in the same workspace, numerous organisations ended up **to offer** fully remote or hybrid contracts. Towards the mid-2010s, working from home tended **growing** steadily in popularity, before accelerating dramatically during the global health crisis. When asked what motivated them **choosing** remote arrangements, employees typically mention things like not **to feel** constantly under observation, or their managers making them **to travel** great distances for meetings that could easily have been handled online. Whatever causes professionals **preferring** flexible models, they have to keep **to adapt** to evolving workplace expectations. Many workers resent **to have** to justify their output when their supervisors cannot manage **finding** the confidence to trust their teams. Remote working may have become a technical reality, but the cultural shift it demands is still very much a work in progress.
items.answer: 1 to get, 2 allow, 3 to stay, 4 offering, 5 to grow, 6 to choose, 7 feeling, 8 travel, 9 to prefer, 10 adapting, 11 having, 12 to find

sections.title: G: Complete with the correct form of the verb in bold
sections.instructions: Complete by writing the verb in bold in the correct form in each pair of sentences.

items.sentence: 1 I wish you'd stop **tell** ...... everyone you've already been offered the job – nothing has been confirmed yet.
2 The facilitator had to stop **tell** ...... two participants to lower their voices before the session could continue.
3 When you're passing the pharmacy, remember **get** ...... some painkillers – we've completely run out.
4 I clearly remember **get** ...... out of the taxi and walking towards the entrance, but everything after that is a blur.
5 We deeply regret **announce** ...... that the evening performance has been cancelled due to unforeseen circumstances.
6 He later regretted **announce** ...... his retirement so publicly before informing his own family.
7 Despite repeated interruptions, the keynote speaker simply went on **talk** ...... as though nothing had happened.
8 He described the company's history and then went on **talk** ...... about the vision for the coming decade.
9 I can't believe it – I completely forgot **go** ...... to the bank to transfer those funds.
10 How can you have forgotten **go** ...... to Edinburgh? We spent a whole week there last summer!
11 The laptop kept overheating, so I tried **change** ...... the power settings, but the problem persisted.
12 Every time I try **change** ...... the screen brightness on this device, it resets to the default.
13 We like **have** ...... the boiler serviced every autumn, just to make sure everything is working safely.
14 She really doesn't like **have** ...... her concentration broken while she's working.

sections.title: H: Complete the second sentence
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: 1 After three failed attempts, she finally managed to pass the driving test. **in** On her third attempt, she finally ...... the driving test.
2 Beatriz can guide tour groups professionally because she holds a certified guiding licence. **to** Beatriz's certified guiding licence ...... tour groups professionally.
3 I was surprised when they put me forward to present the findings at the annual conference. **nominated** I couldn't believe it when they ...... the findings at the annual conference.
4 I had a locker partner at school when I was a teenager and I rather enjoyed it. **like** I used ...... a locker with my classmate when I was at school.
5 On moving to a new city, Daniel made a firm decision not to repeat the same mistakes. **to** On moving to a new city, Daniel resolved ...... the same mistakes again.
6 Are you sorry that you recommended Marco for the promotion? **regret** Do ...... Marco for the promotion?
7 The plan is that you will be collected by the local coordinator. **for** We ...... by the local coordinator.
items.answer: have arranged for you to be collected

items.sentence: 8 Where did you get the idea for designing such a groundbreaking product in your latest collection? **to** What ...... such a groundbreaking product in your latest collection?
1 When she mentioned ...... on Saturdays without extra pay, I immediately threatened to resign. A me to working B me to work C me work D me working
2 The director insisted that all department heads ...... available throughout the audit. A have been B be C are being D being
3 Didn't you ...... that Oliver wasn't at his own leaving party? A think it strange B think strange C think strangely D think it strangely
4 It is absolutely crucial that the emergency response team ...... the moment anything changes. A be it notified B being notified C were notified D be notified
5 I consider ...... to remind the committee that several members have raised serious objections. A my duty B this my duty C it my duty D that my duty
6 Given the look on his face at this morning's briefing, I didn't ...... him about the complaints I had received. A dare telling B dare it telling C dare tell D dare to telling
7 Don't you ...... that Nathan and Priya have been close friends for over twenty years? A incredibly find B find incredible C find incredibly D find it incredible
8 You really shouldn't have let Sophie ...... without clearing the air between you first. A leave B to leave C leaving D to leaving
9 It was rather awkward when the examiner caught us ...... during the written assessment. A to cheat B cheating C cheat D to cheating
10 It's cruel to make a small child ...... in a hot vehicle on such a warm afternoon. A to wait B waiting C wait D for waiting

sections.title: J: Write one word in each gap
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: 'You've been trying (1) ...... find a language exchange partner for far too long,' my friends told me. 'Can't you feel (2) ...... getting rusty? Don't you (3) ...... it exciting to think about becoming truly bilingual?' they asked. They recommended (4) ...... to check out something called a 'Language Café'. A week later I found (5) ...... seated at a long table in a cosy café with thirty other people clutching name badges and pocket phrasebooks. The idea with Language Cafés is that you chat for fifteen minutes, then a bell rings and you move to a new partner. You keep (6) ...... rotating like that all evening, practising whatever language you're learning. So, there I was as a stream of enthusiastic learners passed before me. I didn't (7) ...... admit that I had barely studied Spanish since school, so I pretended to be more fluent than I was. Everyone clearly found (8) ...... hard to sustain a conversation beyond basic pleasantries – well, what do you say to impress someone in fifteen minutes? The Marias, Josés and Alejandros came and went, and I (9) ...... to feel like I couldn't (10) ...... to hear another round of 'How long have you been learning?' Language Cafés might suit some people, but I think I'll stick to online apps to find my inner linguist.
items.answer: 1 to, 2 yourself, 3 find/think/consider, 4 me, 5 myself, 6 on, 7 dare, 8 it, 9 started/began, 10 stand/bear
